title: Goal nudge fires stale after daily goal is met in the same session
description: scheduleDailyGoalNudge is a one-shot notification scheduled at sync time (app foreground / launch). If the user opens the app, the nudge is scheduled for 20:30 because done < goal at that moment. If the user then answers enough questions to meet or exceed their daily goal within that same session — never backgrounding and re-foregrounding the app — no syncReminders call is made, so the pending notification is never cancelled and re-evaluated. At 20:30 iOS delivers the nudge anyway, falsely telling the user they have not met their goal. syncReminders is only triggered on scenePhase == .active (GPLX2026App.swift:175-177) and from explicit settings changes; there is no call to syncReminders (or even a targeted cancelAll) from the study-session completion paths that call recordStudyActivity() (ProgressStore.swift:451).
fix: After the daily goal is met — specifically inside recordStudyActivity() or in any session-completion handler that drives todayProgress.done over the threshold — call UNUserNotificationCenter.current().removePendingNotificationRequests(withIdentifiers: [NotificationManager.goalNudgeID]) (exposing goalNudgeID as internal). Alternatively, call a lightweight NotificationManager.cancelGoalNudgeIfGoalMet(progressStore:) that checks done >= goal and removes only that identifier, without a full sync.

verdict.reasoning: The bug is real and unguarded. scheduleDailyGoalNudge (NotificationManager.swift:140-169) reads progressStore.todayProgress once at sync time, then schedules a one-shot UNCalendarNotificationTrigger for 20:30 today. The only callers of syncReminders are: the splash-screen task (launch), the scenePhase .active transition (foreground re-entry), and settings-change callbacks in SettingsView/OnboardingView. No study-session completion path touches NotificationManager. recordQuestionAnswer (ProgressStore.swift:441) calls only recordStudyActivity(), which increments the UserDefaults activity counter and returns — zero interaction with UNUserNotificationCenter. recordExamResult, recordSimulationResult, and recordHazardResult are likewise silent on notifications. If a user opens the app before 20:30 with done < goal, the nudge is armed for 20:30. If they then meet or exceed their daily goal entirely within that foreground session — never backgrounding — the pending notification is never cancelled or re-evaluated, and iOS delivers it at 20:30 with a message that is factually stale ("Take a few minutes to finish!" when the goal is already met). There is no guard, threshold observer, or goal-met hook anywhere in the codebase that would cancel the goalNudgeID notification intra-session.

title: examCountdownEnabled stays true in AppStorage after exam date is cleared, silently re-arming countdown for the next date
description: The 'Xoá ngày thi' button (lines 232-235) calls progressStore.setExamDate(nil) and then syncReminders(). It does NOT reset examCountdownEnabled to false. scheduleExamCountdown receives nil and returns immediately (NotificationManager.swift:113), so no countdown is scheduled right now. However, examCountdownEnabled remains true in AppStorage. Later, when the user sets a new exam date (via the inline DatePicker binding setter on line 204 or via ExamDateSheet.onConfirm on line 404), syncReminders() is called with examCountdownEnabled still true. Countdown notifications for the brand-new exam date are therefore scheduled automatically, without the user having explicitly toggled the countdown switch on for the new date. The toggle re-appears in the UI as ON (because the examDate guard at line 220 is now satisfied), giving the impression the user opted in for the new exam date.
fix: In the 'Xoá ngày thi' button handler (after progressStore.setExamDate(nil)), also set examCountdownEnabled = false before calling syncReminders(). This mirrors the resetOnDenied pattern used in handleReminderChange and keeps the toggle's persisted state consistent with the absence of an exam date.

verdict.reasoning:
The issue is real and confirmed by direct code reading. The exact mechanism:

1. examCountdownEnabled is @AppStorage (line 17) — it persists across sessions and is never reset to false when the exam date is cleared.

2. The "Xoá ngày thi" button (lines 232-235) calls progressStore.setExamDate(nil) and syncReminders(), but does NOT set examCountdownEnabled = false.

3. syncReminders() (lines 476-487) passes examCountdownEnabled (still true) to NotificationManager.syncReminders(). That function reaches the check at line 72: "if examCountdownEnabled { await scheduleExamCountdown(examDate: progressStore.examDate) }". scheduleExamCountdown receives nil and returns immediately at line 113 "guard let examDate else { return }". So no countdown is scheduled NOW — the state is safe at this moment.

4. However, examCountdownEnabled remains true in AppStorage. The toggle widget (lines 220-230) becomes invisible because it is wrapped in "if progressStore.examDate != nil" (line 220), so the user has no way to see or flip the persisted true value.

5. When the user later sets a new exam date — via either the inline DatePicker binding setter (line 204: "set: { progressStore.setExamDate($0); syncReminders() }") or ExamDateSheet.onConfirm (line 940-941 + line 404) — syncReminders() is called again. This time progressStore.examDate is non-nil, so scheduleExamCountdown fires for the new date with no further user action.

6. The toggle then re-appears (examDate != nil is now satisfied) and renders in the ON state, making it appear as if the user previously opted in for this new date.

There is no guard in any of these paths that resets examCountdownEnabled to false when the exam date is removed. The nil-guard in scheduleExamCountdown is a scheduling-time guard only — it does not address the persisted preference flag. The issue is not by design (there is no comment or pattern suggesting "carry the preference forward to the next date is intentional"), not a compile claim, and not negligible — it results in countdown notifications being silently armed for a new exam date without explicit user consent.

title: isMedium boundary excludes iPad Pro 12.9" portrait (1024 pt), triggering 3-panel landscape layout in portrait
description: isMedium is defined as `windowWidth >= 744 && windowWidth < 1024`. iPad Pro 12.9" in portrait reports exactly 1024 pt, which fails the `< 1024` guard, so it falls through to isWide (`>= 1024`). The 3-panel 'landscape' layout and its associated metrics (32 pt content padding, 16 pt grid spacing, 3 columns) then fire in portrait, even though the code comments for isMedium say 'iPad portrait'. Every other iPad (11", Air, mini) in portrait is < 1024 pt and hits isMedium correctly. Only the 12.9" Pro in portrait is misclassified.
fix: Change the isMedium upper bound to include 1024 pt: `var isMedium: Bool { windowWidth >= 744 && windowWidth <= 1024 }` and change isWide to `windowWidth > 1024`. This keeps all iPad portrait orientations in isMedium and reserves isWide for landscape-only widths. Alternatively use `<= 1024` for isMedium and `> 1024` for isWide.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and unguarded. The key facts:

1. isMedium at line 15: `windowWidth >= 744 && windowWidth < 1024` — strict less-than means 1024 pt fails this check.

2. isWide at line 18: `horizontalSizeClass == .regular && windowWidth >= 1024` — the claim overlooks this guard, but the guard does NOT save the situation. iPads report `.regular` horizontal size class in BOTH portrait and landscape when running full-screen. Apple's UIKit documentation confirms this: iPad always has `.regular` horizontal size class in full-screen mode regardless of orientation. So on an iPad Pro 12.9" in portrait at 1024 pt width, both conditions in isWide are satisfied.

3. Consequence: BaseExamView.swift lines 139-195 (labeled "iPad landscape: 3-panel") fires in portrait on the 12.9" Pro. The 3-panel HStack with 40/35/25 column split, 32 pt content padding, 16 pt grid spacing, and a question-grid sidebar renders in portrait mode. QuestionView.swift line 105 and SplitContent.swift line 11 take the same isWide branch. All of these use the term "landscape" in their comments but fire based purely on the width+sizeClass condition, which 12.9" portrait satisfies.

4. No orientation guard, no device-type guard, and no alternate breakpoint exists anywhere in LayoutMetrics.swift or its callers to handle this case.

5. Every other iPad (11-inch, Air, mini) has a portrait width under 1024 pt and correctly falls into isMedium. Only the 12.9" Pro's exact 1024 pt portrait width is misclassified as isWide.

The fix would be to change isMedium to `windowWidth >= 744 && windowWidth < 1024` → `windowWidth >= 744 && windowWidth <= 1024` (inclusive upper bound) or to `windowWidth < 1025`, so the 12.9" portrait case is captured by isMedium rather than falling through to isWide. There is no existing guard that prevents this misclassification.

title: topicProgressSidebar LazyVGrid has no ScrollView — overflows and clips for large question sets on iPad
description: topicProgressSidebar (called at line 145 in the isWide 3-panel branch) is a VStack containing a LazyVGrid that renders a cell for every question in `allQuestions`. There is no ScrollView around this VStack. For topics with many questions — 'Tất cả câu hỏi' has up to 600 entries for B2 — the grid alone reaches 5 040 pt (120 rows × 42 pt), far exceeding any iPad screen height. The VStack overflows its frame silently: cells beyond the visible area are drawn outside the clipped region, and the Spacer + summary text at the bottom of the sidebar are never reached. This is actively reproducible on iPad landscape using any broad topic key.
fix: Wrap the VStack in `ScrollView { ... }` at line 422. Apply `.clipped()` to the outer `.frame(width: rightWidth)` call at line 147 to prevent overflow bleeding. Alternatively cap sidebar entries (e.g. show only a window of ±25 questions around currentIndex) to avoid the ScrollView-inside-GeometryReader height ambiguity.

verdict.reasoning: The code at lines 419–480 of QuestionView.swift confirms the claim exactly. topicProgressSidebar is a plain VStack (no ScrollView, no .clipped(), no maxHeight frame) containing a LazyVGrid that iterates every element of allQuestions via ForEach(0..<allQuestions.count). At the call site (line 145–146) it only receives .frame(width: rightWidth) — no height constraint. The parent HStack(alignment: .top) inside the GeometryReader gives each child its natural (intrinsic) vertical size; nothing clamps or scrolls the sidebar column. For topics with large question sets (e.g. 600 questions in a 5-column grid at minHeight 36 + 6 pt spacing = 42 pt per row), the grid alone computes to ~5,040 pt, which overflows any iPad screen height (~820–1024 pt usable). The Spacer() and anything below the LazyVGrid within the VStack will be pushed entirely off-screen and are unreachable. There is no guard, no alternative path, and no clipping that would contain or mitigate this. The left and center columns each have their own ScrollView (lines 117 and 132), making the asymmetry with the sidebar intentional-looking but erroneous. The bug is real, reproducible on any broad topic key in iPad landscape (isWide branch), and the bottom summary content is structurally unreachable.

title: Duplicate close buttons on iPad portrait in HazardTestView: system nav bar xmark and custom overlay xmark both visible and overlapping
description: The body applies `.toolbarVisibility(isCurrentlyLandscape ? .hidden : .visible, for: .navigationBar)` (line 52) and places an xmark ToolbarItem in `.navigationBarLeading` (line 55). `testContent` branches on `isLandscape || isRegular` (line 106): on iPad portrait `isRegular` is true but `isCurrentlyLandscape` is false, so `landscapeLayout` is used while the navigation bar stays visible. `landscapeLayout` always renders its own custom close button inside the ZStack overlay (line 165) with `.padding(.top, 42)`. With `.ignoresSafeArea()` on the ZStack (line 297), this custom button sits at y≈42 pt from the screen top — exactly within the navigation bar's bounding box (≈50 pt tall) — causing both buttons to be visible and physically overlapping on every iPad in portrait orientation.
fix: Suppress the in-overlay custom xmark when the navigation bar is already shown. Change the custom close Button at line 163-173 to be conditional: `if isCurrentlyLandscape { Button { showExitDialog = true } label: { ... } }`. This keeps the custom button only for the landscape/fullscreen case where the nav bar is hidden, and lets the ToolbarItem xmark handle portrait and other cases without duplication.

verdict.reasoning:
The code confirms all three conditions the claim depends on, and no guard neutralizes the conflict.

1. Navigation bar visibility on iPad portrait: Line 52 uses `isCurrentlyLandscape ? .hidden : .visible`. On iPad portrait `isCurrentlyLandscape` is false (confirmed by the `onGeometryChange` update at lines 112–116 which only sets it true when `width > height`). So the system navigation bar — including its xmark ToolbarItem at `.navigationBarLeading` (lines 54–58) — stays visible.

2. Layout routing on iPad portrait: Line 106 branches on `isLandscape || isRegular`. On iPad, `horizontalSizeClass` is `.regular` in portrait, so `isRegular` is true, and `landscapeLayout` is always called regardless of actual orientation.

3. Custom close button position: Inside `landscapeLayout`, the "Top bar overlay" HStack (lines 163–210) contains a custom xmark Button (lines 165–173). The HStack is given `.padding(.top, 42)` (line 209) and `.frame(maxHeight: .infinity, alignment: .top)` (line 210). The outer ZStack has `.ignoresSafeArea()` (line 297), meaning coordinates are measured from the physical screen top, not from below the nav bar. iOS nav bars on modern iPads are approximately 50 pt tall, so the custom button at y≈42 pt sits entirely within the nav bar's bounding box.

No conditional hides the custom button when the nav bar is present. There is no `if !isCurrentlyLandscape` guard around the top-bar HStack, no `.opacity(0)`, no `isHidden` modifier, nothing. The two xmark buttons — the system toolbar item and the custom overlay button — are simultaneously rendered and physically overlapping on every iPad in portrait orientation. The issue is real, not negligible, and not by design (the intent is clearly to show the custom bar only in full-screen/landscape mode where the nav bar is hidden).

title: safeWrite captures non-Sendable types in a @Sendable DispatchQueue closure
description: The compiler (Swift 6, SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY: complete) emits two confirmed warnings: "capture of 'block' with non-Sendable type '(UserDefaults) -> Void' in a '@Sendable' closure" and "capture of 'defaults' with non-Sendable type 'UserDefaults' in a '@Sendable' closure". The dispatch closure passed to writeQueue.async is @Sendable, but both the `block` parameter and the `defaults` capture are non-Sendable. While UserDefaults is documented as thread-safe, the `block` closure type is unconstrained — callers could accidentally capture @MainActor-isolated or otherwise non-Sendable mutable state in the closure they pass to safeWrite. This is a real Swift 6 data-safety hole that the compiler has already flagged.
fix: Mark the block parameter @Sendable: `func safeWrite(_ block: @escaping @Sendable (UserDefaults) -> Void)`. This makes the compiler enforce that every closure passed to safeWrite is itself Sendable, preventing accidental captures of non-Sendable types at each call site. For UserDefaults, no annotation change is needed — it is safe across threads in practice, and adding @Sendable to the closure suppresses the second warning as well.

verdict.reasoning:
A clean xcodebuild run (clean + build) against the actual project reproduces both warnings verbatim at ProgressStore.swift:39 — exactly as claimed:

  • "capture of 'block' with non-Sendable type '(UserDefaults) -> Void' in a '@Sendable' closure"
  • "capture of 'defaults' with non-Sendable type 'UserDefaults' in a '@Sendable' closure"

These are emitted by Swift 6.3.2 under SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY: complete (confirmed in project.yml line 44). An incremental build suppressed them because the file was unchanged since last compile; a clean build surfaces them unambiguously.

There is no guard, no @unchecked Sendable annotation, no nonisolated marker, and no @Sendable constraint on the block parameter — nothing in the code suppresses or works around the issue. The fix would be marking block as @escaping @Sendable (UserDefaults) -> Void, which would also force all call sites to pass only Sendable-safe closures and surface any real data-safety problems at the call sites.

The claim that callers could accidentally capture @MainActor-isolated or otherwise non-Sendable mutable state is also correct: because block is unconstrained, the Swift 6 type system does not enforce Sendability for anything captured inside it, so the warning is both real and meaningful, not negligible.

title: getOrCreateKey TOCTOU: errSecDuplicateItem returns wrong (transient) key, corrupting encrypted data
description: getOrCreateKey (1) loads from Keychain — returns nil on first install, (2) generates a fresh SymmetricKey, (3) calls saveKeyToKeychain. saveKeyToKeychain treats errSecDuplicateItem as success (line 76) and returns. Control then falls to line 50 `return newKey` — the locally-generated key, NOT the key already stored in the Keychain. This matters when two concurrent callers race through step 1 before either has completed step 3: the main thread calls SecureStorage.load (e.g. in the examHistory getter, line 158 of ProgressStore) and simultaneously a writeQueue task calls SecureStorage.save. Both find an empty Keychain, both generate different keys. The one that wins the SecItemAdd stores its key; the loser gets errSecDuplicateItem and returns its own different transient key. Any data encrypted under that transient key (e.g. a history record written in the same session) is permanently unreadable after the next launch, when getOrCreateKey reads the winner's key from the Keychain.
fix: After errSecDuplicateItem, re-read the Keychain to return the key that is actually stored: `if status == errSecDuplicateItem { if let stored = loadKeyFromKeychain() { return stored } }`. This eliminates the TOCTOU by making the loser of the race use the same key as the winner rather than a shadow key.

verdict.reasoning:
The TOCTOU race is confirmed by direct code inspection with no guard present.

getOrCreateKey (SecureStorage.swift:46-51) is an unprotected check-then-act sequence: (1) loadKeyFromKeychain returns nil on first install, (2) a fresh SymmetricKey is generated, (3) saveKeyToKeychain is called, (4) return newKey. saveKeyToKeychain (line 76) treats errSecDuplicateItem as success and returns without re-reading the key already committed to the Keychain.

Two concurrent callers are created by the real call pattern in ProgressStore: the examHistory getter (line 158) calls SecureStorage.load → getOrCreateKey on whichever thread reads the @Observable property (typically the main thread from SwiftUI), while safeWrite dispatches SecureStorage.save → getOrCreateKey onto writeQueue, a background serial DispatchQueue. ProgressStore has no @MainActor annotation, so these two threads genuinely run concurrently.

There is no synchronization at any level: SecureStorage has no lock, no actor, no serial queue, and no retry-read-after-errSecDuplicateItem path. On the first app launch when the Keychain entry is absent, both callers can pass the loadKeyFromKeychain nil check before either completes SecItemAdd. The loser of the SecItemAdd race receives errSecDuplicateItem, treats it as success, and returns its own locally-generated key — which is different from the key now stored in the Keychain. Any data encrypted under that transient key (e.g. a history record written in that same session) becomes permanently unreadable after relaunch, when getOrCreateKey correctly reads the winner's key from the Keychain.

The race window is narrow (first launch only, sub-millisecond), but the consequence is silent, permanent data corruption with no error surfaced to the user. No existing guard, fallback, or migration path prevents or recovers from it.

title: UIScreen.main.bounds used for confetti width — deprecated and wrong in multi-window/Stage Manager
description: `UIScreen.main.bounds.width` is deprecated since iOS 16 and returns incorrect values in Split View, Slide Over, and Stage Manager (which iPads running iOS 18 use). The confetti particles are pinned at absolute x positions computed from the wrong screen width, so they can fall off-screen or cluster on one side in multi-window contexts. `UIScreen.main` itself is also deprecated as a property in iOS 16+.
fix: Use the `GeometryReader`'s `geo.size.width` that is already in scope in `CelebrationOverlay.body` instead of `UIScreen.main.bounds.width`. Move the particle generation into `.onAppear` receiving the geometry size, or pass the width as a parameter.

verdict.reasoning: The code at line 29 uses `UIScreen.main.bounds.width` to compute random x-positions for confetti particles, but the CelebrationOverlay is applied as an `.overlay{}` on a content view (line 65 of DailyGoalCelebrationModifier) — its actual rendered width is constrained to that content view's bounds, which a GeometryReader already captures as `geo.size.width` (geo is in scope but unused for x). On iPad in Split View, Slide Over, or Stage Manager (all available on iOS 18 which is the deployment target), `UIScreen.main.bounds.width` returns the full physical display width while the overlay may be only half or less of that. Particles are spawned at x-coordinates spread up to the full screen width but must render inside the narrower window frame — those beyond `geo.size.width` fall off the edge of the overlay, making confetti appear to bunch on the left side or vanish. No guard, conditional, or workaround exists. The `UIScreen.main` API is also formally deprecated since iOS 16. The fix is to use `geo.size.width` instead, which is already present in scope.
